title: Trauma and the Stomach: Nausea, Knots, and the Gut-Brain Axis
excerpt: Explore how trauma impacts the stomach through the gut-brain axis, causing nausea, knots, and chronic discomfort. Understand body-based healing.
body:
Introduction: When Trauma Grips the Gut

Many people who have experienced trauma notice something peculiar: their stomach reacts before their mind even registers the stress. That knot in the gut, that sudden nausea, or the chronic digestive issues—these aren’t just psychosomatic clichés or “all in your head” symptoms. They are very real, deeply wired responses that illustrate the powerful connection between trauma and the digestive system. This article explores the science behind trauma’s impact on the stomach, focusing on the gut-brain axis, and offers insights into somatic healing approaches that can help regulate this often overlooked but vital body-mind connection.

The Gut-Brain Axis: A Two-Way Street

The gut and brain communicate constantly via a complex system called the gut-brain axis. This includes neural pathways (notably the vagus nerve), hormonal signaling, and immune system interactions. According to Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory, the vagus nerve plays a central role in regulating our physiological state and emotional responses. When trauma activates our nervous system’s survival mode, it disrupts this communication, leading to symptoms like nausea and stomach tightness.

Research led by Emeran Mayer, a pioneer in neurogastroenterology, demonstrates that the gut contains its own nervous system—the enteric nervous system—often called the “second brain.” It can operate independently but remains in constant dialogue with the brain via the vagus nerve and other pathways. Trauma can dysregulate this dialogue, affecting motility, secretion, and immune responses in the gut, which may explain why trauma survivors often experience gastrointestinal symptoms.

Key Points on Gut-Brain Axis and Trauma

  • The vagus nerve links the brain and the gut, modulating digestion and emotional states (Porges, 2011).
  • Trauma-induced autonomic dysregulation can impair vagal tone, causing gut distress (Dana, 2018).
  • Chronic stress and trauma alter gut microbiota, compounding digestive symptoms (Mayer, 2016).

Why Trauma Feels Like a Knot in the Stomach

When trauma triggers the autonomic nervous system’s fight, flight, or freeze responses, the body shifts blood flow away from the digestive system to prepare for survival actions. This physiological response can cause sensations described as “knots” or “tightness” in the stomach. Bessel van der Kolk, in his seminal work The Body Keeps the Score, explains how trauma is held not just in memory but in bodily sensations, including gastrointestinal discomfort.

Pat Ogden, a leader in sensorimotor psychotherapy, emphasizes that these sensations are crucial clues for trauma survivors. They represent the body’s attempt to process and contain overwhelming experiences. Ignoring these signals or trying to intellectualize trauma without addressing the somatic component can leave people feeling stuck and disconnected from their healing process.

Nausea and Trauma: More Than Just Anxiety

Nausea is a common complaint among trauma survivors, often dismissed as anxiety or panic. However, the physiological basis is more complex. Dan Siegel’s work on interpersonal neurobiology highlights how emotional distress activates brain areas like the insula and anterior cingulate cortex, which process visceral sensations, including nausea.

Francine Shapiro, who developed EMDR therapy, notes that trauma processing often includes working through physical symptoms such as nausea because they reflect unresolved neural patterns linked to the traumatic event. Simply put, nausea can be a somatic manifestation of trauma that signals the nervous system is dysregulated and stuck in a threat response pattern.

The Role of the Enteric Nervous System and Immune Response

The enteric nervous system (ENS) contains about 100 million neurons—more than the spinal cord—lining the gastrointestinal tract. It regulates digestion and communicates with the central nervous system. Trauma can cause ENS dysfunction, leading to symptoms like irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), bloating, and cramping.

Gabor Maté highlights how chronic stress and trauma impact the immune system, which is heavily involved in gut health. Inflammatory responses triggered by trauma can exacerbate gut symptoms, creating a feedback loop that maintains both physical and emotional distress.

Somatic Healing Approaches for Gut-Related Trauma Symptoms

Understanding the gut-brain axis opens doors for somatic healing techniques that do more than talk through trauma—they work directly with the body to regulate the nervous system.

1. Polyvagal-Informed Practices

Deb Dana’s work translates Porges’ Polyvagal Theory into practical exercises that help regulate the vagus nerve. Techniques like paced breathing, gentle movement, and vocalization can enhance vagal tone, soothing the stomach and calming the nervous system.

2. Sensorimotor Psychotherapy and Body Awareness

Pat Ogden’s sensorimotor approach encourages tracking bodily sensations—like knots or nausea—to access trauma stored in the body. This helps clients build a sense of safety and regain control over their internal states.

3. Mindful Movement and Breathwork

Practices like yoga, tai chi, or simple diaphragmatic breathing stimulate the parasympathetic nervous system and improve gut motility, reducing discomfort and reconnecting the mind and body.

4. Nutrition and Microbiome Support

Emeran Mayer and others underscore the importance of gut microbiota in mental health. A trauma-informed nutritional approach that supports microbiome diversity can reduce inflammation and improve gut-brain communication.

Healing the Gut-Brain Axis: A Holistic View

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems model offers a framework to work with the different parts of ourselves, including those that hold trauma in the body. Integrating somatic therapies with psychological approaches can untangle the complex web between trauma and gut symptoms.

Ultimately, healing trauma-related stomach issues requires a shift from trying to “fix” symptoms to learning how to listen and respond to the body’s signals. Somatic work invites us to become curious about those knots and nausea, understanding them as messages from a nervous system that needs regulation and compassion.

“The body keeps the score. It remembers what the mind tries to forget. Trauma lives in the gut as much as in the brain.” — Bessel van der Kolk

Conclusion: From Stuck to Regulated

If you’ve tried therapy and still feel stuck when your stomach tightens or you get that wave of nausea, it’s not because you’re failing. It’s because trauma is deeply embodied, especially in the gut-brain axis. Science shows us that healing requires more than just talking—it requires engaging the nervous system through somatic practices, breath, movement, and body awareness.

By understanding how trauma impacts your stomach and gut, you reclaim agency over your sensations and symptoms. This awareness is the first step toward regulation, integration, and true healing.

faqs.q: Why does trauma cause stomach knots and nausea?
faqs.a: Trauma activates the autonomic nervous system’s survival response, shifting blood flow away from digestion and disrupting gut-brain communication. This causes sensations like knots and nausea as the body reacts to perceived threat.

faqs.q: Can gut symptoms from trauma be treated without medication?
faqs.a: Yes. Somatic therapies, breathwork, mindfulness, and nutrition-focused approaches can regulate the nervous system and improve gut health without relying solely on medication.

faqs.q: How does the vagus nerve influence gut reactions to trauma?
faqs.a: The vagus nerve is the main communication highway between the brain and gut. Trauma can reduce vagal tone, impairing this communication and leading to digestive symptoms like nausea and tightness.

faqs.q: What somatic practices help regulate trauma-related gut symptoms?
faqs.a: Polyvagal-informed breathing, sensorimotor psychotherapy, mindful movement, and body awareness exercises help soothe the nervous system and restore healthy gut-brain communication.
